title: From Corporate Careers to Restroom Revolution: The UNICORN Story
guestBio: Denielle Finkelstein and Thyme Sullivan are cousins and co-founders of UNICORN, a company revolutionizing restroom equity through innovative product dispensers. They left successful corporate careers to build a mission-driven business now serving Fortune 500 companies including Chase, American Express, and major airports across the country.
excerpt: Two cousins spotted a gap most of us overlook in restroom access and product equity, then built a movement around it. Discover how Denielle and Thyme transformed an invisible problem into a Fortune 500 solution.
metaDescription: Learn how UNICORN co-CEOs Denielle Finkelstein and Thyme Sullivan transformed restroom equity into a Fortune 500 business serving Chase and AmEx.
keyTakeaways: Leadership often starts with noticing what others overlook rather than inventing something completely new.
Building a business with family requires clear boundaries and defined roles to maintain both relationships and success.
Mission-driven companies can scale to Fortune 500 clients when they solve real problems with thoughtful solutions.
Restroom equity is about dignity, wellness, and inclusion, not just convenience.
Transforming invisible gaps into business opportunities requires courage to challenge the status quo.
Co-CEO partnerships can work when both leaders bring complementary strengths and shared vision.
Scaling a values-based company requires maintaining your core mission while adapting to larger market demands.

pullQuotes.text: Leadership isn't always about inventing something new — sometimes it's about noticing what's been ignored, and daring to redesign it.

timestamps.label: Building a business as cousins and co-CEOs
Redesigning restrooms for dignity and inclusion

content:
What if the next big business opportunity is hiding in plain sight, in a place you visit multiple times every day but rarely think about improving? That's exactly what happened when cousins Denielle Finkelstein and Thyme Sullivan looked at restrooms with fresh eyes and saw not just a functional space, but an opportunity to create dignity, wellness, and true equity.

In this powerful episode of The Manage Her, Aimee sits down with the co-founders and co-CEOs of UNICORN, a company that's revolutionizing restroom access and product equity with sleek, thoughtful dispensers now found in Fortune 500 companies like Chase and American Express, plus airports across the country. Their story is a masterclass in transforming invisible problems into world-changing solutions.

The Power of Noticing What Others Overlook

The UNICORN story begins with a simple but profound realization: restroom equity isn't just about having facilities available, it's about ensuring those facilities serve everyone with dignity and inclusion. While most people might notice an inconvenience and move on, Denielle and Thyme saw a systemic gap that affected millions of people daily.

This is where true leadership often begins. You don't always need to invent something completely revolutionary. Sometimes the most impactful businesses come from paying attention to what's been ignored, overlooked, or accepted as "just the way things are." The cousins recognized that restrooms could be redesigned as hubs of wellness and inclusion rather than afterthoughts in building design.

Their journey from corporate careers to entrepreneurship wasn't just about starting a business—it was about building a movement. When you spot a problem that affects people's basic dignity and daily experience, you have a choice: accept it or act on it. Denielle and Thyme chose to act.

Building Success Through Family Partnership

One of the most fascinating aspects of the UNICORN story is how two family members successfully navigate being co-CEOs. Building a business with family brings unique challenges and opportunities that most entrepreneurs never face. The key to their success lies in establishing clear boundaries, defined roles, and maintaining open communication about both business decisions and family dynamics.

Their partnership demonstrates that co-leadership can work when both parties bring complementary strengths and maintain a shared vision. Rather than competing for control, they've created a structure where their different skills and perspectives strengthen the company's foundation. This approach has allowed them to scale from startup to serving major corporations while maintaining their core mission and family relationship.

Working with family in business requires a level of emotional intelligence and boundary-setting that goes beyond typical business partnerships. You're not just building a company together—you're protecting relationships that extend far beyond the boardroom. Their success shows that when done thoughtfully, family businesses can leverage deep trust and shared values to create something truly impactful.

Scaling Mission-Driven Business to Fortune 500 Clients

Getting your product into companies like Chase, American Express, and major airports doesn't happen overnight. It requires demonstrating that your mission-driven solution also delivers business value. UNICORN's success in scaling to Fortune 500 spaces proves that companies focused on equity and inclusion can compete at the highest levels when they solve real problems with thoughtful, well-designed solutions.

The key insight here is that mission and profit don't have to be opposing forces. When you create products that genuinely improve people's experiences and address real needs, businesses take notice. Large corporations are increasingly recognizing that employee and customer wellness directly impact their bottom line, making solutions like UNICORN's not just socially responsible choices, but smart business decisions.

Scaling a values-based company requires maintaining your core mission while adapting to larger market demands. This means staying true to your why while being flexible in your how. UNICORN's growth demonstrates that you can expand your reach without compromising your values—in fact, strong values can become your competitive advantage in the marketplace.

Redefining Dignity in Everyday Spaces

The conversation reveals a powerful truth about leadership and innovation: sometimes the most transformative changes happen in the spaces we use most but think about least. By reimagining restrooms as places of dignity, wellness, and inclusion, UNICORN is changing how we think about everyday infrastructure and its impact on human experience.

This approach to business—focusing on dignity and inclusion in overlooked spaces—offers a blueprint for finding opportunities in your own industry. Where are the gaps in dignity? What experiences do people endure rather than enjoy? What problems have become so normalized that they're invisible? These questions can lead you to business opportunities that create real value while making the world more equitable.

When you design solutions with dignity at the center, you're not just creating products—you're creating experiences that affirm people's worth and meet their real needs. This human-centered approach to business building ensures that your success contributes to a more inclusive world.

The UNICORN story reminds us that leadership isn't always about grand gestures or revolutionary inventions. Sometimes it's about having the courage to notice what others have ignored and the determination to build something better. Whether you're considering leaving corporate life to start something new or looking for ways to make your current role more impactful, remember that the biggest opportunities often hide in the smallest, most overlooked details of daily life.

Your next big idea might be waiting in a space you visit every day but never really see. The question is: are you ready to notice it and do something about it?

quiz.title: What's Your Hidden Opportunity Superpower?
quiz.description: Some leaders invent from scratch, while others transform what's already there. Discover how you naturally spot and act on the opportunities others miss.

questions.question: When you're in a public space that feels frustrating or poorly designed, what's your typical response?

options.text: I start mentally redesigning it and wondering why no one has fixed these obvious problems
I think about how I could create something completely different and better from the ground up
I consider who would need to collaborate to make meaningful changes happen here
I analyze what it would actually take to implement improvements and make them stick

questions.question: At work, you're most energized when you're:

options.text: Finding the gaps between what exists and what people actually need
Leading initiatives that haven't been tried before in your industry
Building bridges between different teams, perspectives, or stakeholder groups
Taking promising concepts and developing concrete plans to make them reality

questions.question: When you hear about a successful business, you're most curious about:

options.text: What problem they noticed that everyone else was ignoring or accepting
How they found the courage to do something no one had done before
How they built the relationships and partnerships that made it possible
What systems and strategies they used to grow from idea to success

questions.question: Your friends would say your best leadership quality is:

options.text: You see solutions in places others don't even realize there are problems
You're willing to take risks and try things that might not work
You bring out the best in people and help everyone feel heard and valued
You can take any good idea and figure out how to actually make it happen

questions.question: When working on a challenging project, you naturally focus on:

options.text: Understanding the deeper patterns and root causes behind surface-level issues
Pushing boundaries and exploring approaches that feel fresh and untested
Making sure all voices are included and different perspectives are represented
Creating step-by-step plans that can realistically be executed and sustained

questions.question: The business opportunities that excite you most are ones where you can:

options.text: Transform something ordinary into something that serves people much better
Be among the first to explore completely new territory in your field
Create positive change that brings different communities together
Build something that starts small but has the potential to scale significantly

results.title: The Pattern Spotter
results.description: You have an incredible gift for seeing the connections and systems that others walk right past. Like Denielle and Thyme noticing that restrooms could be wellness hubs, you excel at identifying how existing things could work better for everyone. Your superpower lies in transforming the everyday into the extraordinary. This episode will inspire you to trust those observations you've been having and show you how two women turned a simple insight into a Fortune 500 revolution.

results.title: The Bold Pioneer
results.description: You're naturally drawn to uncharted territory and aren't afraid to be the first one to try something completely new. Your courage to challenge the status quo is your greatest asset, and you inspire others to think bigger about what's possible. While others see obstacles, you see adventures waiting to happen. Listen to this episode to discover how channeling your pioneering spirit into overlooked problems can create massive impact, just like UNICORN did with restroom equity.

results.title: The Relationship Builder
results.description: Your strength lies in understanding people and creating connections that last. You naturally see how to bring different perspectives together to solve problems, and you excel at building trust across diverse groups. Like the co-CEO partnership in this episode, you understand that the best solutions come from combining complementary strengths. This UNICORN story will show you how your collaborative approach can scale into movements that change entire industries.

results.title: The Strategic Amplifier
results.description: You have a talent for taking good ideas and figuring out how to make them work in the real world. While others get stuck in the dreaming phase, you naturally think about scalability, systems, and sustainable growth. Your practical wisdom helps turn vision into lasting impact. This episode will energize you as you hear how UNICORN maintained their mission while scaling to Fortune 500 clients – proving that values-driven businesses can compete at the highest levels.

transcript:
Hi everyone and welcome back to The Manager, the podcast where we eliminate the invisible work women do every day and explore how it translates into powerful leadership and world-changing impact. I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, entrepreneur, author, mama of six, and a firm believer that some of the most important innovations start with what's been overlooked.

Today's guests are the perfect example of that. I first met Denielle Finkelstein and Thyme Sullivan last June at a supplier diversity event with PepsiCo in New York. We instantly connected not just because of their incredible energy, but because of the powerful mission they've built together. They're cousins, they're co-CEOs, and they're co-founders of Unicorn, a company that's reimagining bathroom access and product equity in public and corporate spaces.

What started as a bold idea to bring dignity, visibility, and inclusion into the most unexpected of places has grown into a movement. You've probably seen their sleek, thoughtful dispensers in stadiums, airports, and Fortune 500 headquarters like Chase and American Express. But what you may not see is the story behind it - two women who left successful corporate careers, trusted their vision, and built something that touches culture, wellness, and infrastructure all at once.

Today we're talking about the moment the idea for Unicorn sparked to life, what it's like building a company as a family, and how they're navigating scale, equity, and leadership with both heart and systems thinking. Let's dive in.

**Aimee:** Hi guys. Okay, welcome. So, you both came from impressive corporate careers. What was the moment that sparked the idea for Unicorn and why did you guys decide to build it together?

**Thyme:** Great. Well, thank you, Aimee. So happy to be here. We actually met you at Pepsi and that is kind of the origin of the story. That was my first job out of school. So I spent over 27 years with Pepsi and Coke and Nestle and I kept spending a lot of time in supermarkets and realizing that you could see the ingredients everything from ketchup to cat food but not period products. 

So really the idea came when both of us left our corporate career. So my whole background was in sales and operations and Denielle was at the absolute height of her career in fashion with branding and marketing working everywhere from Ann Taylor coach running international for Kate Spade chief merchant at Talbots. She had this incredible background and we both decided to try to do something new and you're seeing this so much right now in the world that women are getting to a certain point. They worked their whole lives to get to a certain point in their career. And they get there and they say, "Is that it?"

And this was our way to build a legacy to produce products that were better for our daughters and all women was really where it started.

**Aimee:** That's awesome. So, so inspiring. Yeah. So, so you guys are sitting there, you're doing your marketing, Denielle, and you're over at the corporate. And so, okay. So, you guys want to make better tampons, better pads, safer, right? So what do you guys do?

**Denielle:** I think the biggest thing is what we recognized is that we because we have daughters, how do we do something that's different for them generationally? But I think also when we really sat back and we looked at where we were in our careers and very impressive careers, we didn't have any impact. We weren't doing something that had purpose behind it. And I joke my last job was great, but I was making sweaters for 80-year-old women. Those 80-year-old women needed it. Thyme was selling hot pockets. When you really break it down, our purpose - we weren't really leaning into what we could do to change the world for our kids and this next generation.

And so it was about this whole concept of creating a better product. In the beginning of our business, we were a retail business on shelf, really following that grocery store concept and going after being there for the consumer. And this is really I think when we actually recognized that this could be a movement and not just the product anymore was when we listened to our customers and we had customers that were buying our product off shelf and then they would be putting it into a bathroom. 

The reason they were putting into a bathroom in more in this corporate space and offices and even in schools was the quality of our product was what people really resonated with. But it made us sit back and say, "Hey, listen. There's something even bigger than this. We're going to sell a premium product on shelf and that's not going to actually have the impact and the purpose that we really set out to do was to change and advance this next generation."

We sat back and we said, we can do this better. There's something that has to be done. And the system that had been created, and we always joke these machines that are in there, these period product machines have been there since 1980. Actually our team has actually gone back and done the history on them and it's back to 1940. And so those period product dispensers are archaic and they haven't evolved. They're not modern.

And when we think about the workplace in particular, we knew that we had to do something differently. And so this was really one of those moments in business where if we had done this right at the beginning, we wouldn't have gotten to this place that we're at. And it allowed us to really sit back and number one, listen to what our customer and what the feedback had been, but number two was we're going to be bold and we were already bold making the change into doing what we're doing now. But we're going to completely pull out of retail and we're going to shift the entire business to go into the bathroom space and put period products where they actually need to be, which is actually physically in the bathroom stall, just like toilet paper.

And so that was the moment that this changed from it just being a product to actually creating a movement. And more importantly, this is systemic change. This is the change that this next generation, it'll be part of their lives. And so when we think about that now we're really leading with purpose and we're really leading with impact. That's the piece that gets us really excited because those early days it was we wanted a better product but the shift really became once we came into the bathroom space and that really turned it into a movement.

**Aimee:** That's just awesome. I love and I think that this is a I don't know if it's uniquely woman but you definitely see it more in female-led companies where we're looking we listen.

**Denielle:** It's our superpower. We say this.

**Aimee:** It really is. Yeah. Yeah. And I really feel like I was able to do that at Tomahawk too which is really making a big shift in our tech space too based on what the customer needed at the time and it was a big technology shift that was happening when we were getting going during COVID and I think listening is our superpower and I love that you guys are focused on purpose because I think that's such a big part of finding how we're going to really optimize ourselves as people. Where are we really going to find the highest forms of ourselves? And it's once we focus on how our purpose and our impact that we can have on the world, what can we do with our time, what's our legacy going to be?

**Thyme:** Yep.

**Aimee:** It has to be more. It has to be more. That's why I started writing and doing the podcast because I love doing technology, but I don't think it's enough of a legacy for me. And I needed more.

**Denielle:** And so now I write and I do my podcast because I want to promote the women. And I want to show the world what women do as leaders, how we do things differently. And I love you guys talk about how you honor your cycles. You honor your downtime and your rest. And I think that that's something that as we as women, as more mature business owners. If you don't do that, man, you will burn yourself to the ground. And you guys seem to do it real well. That is I think one of the things that when Thyme and I started this that was the commitment we made to each other is that we had to protect ourselves and our mental well-being and our families that had to come first and then the business because in our prior lives, our prior chapters was the job came first and it always did.

And that was really one of those enormous shifts that we did. We made a commitment to one another. We're like, "We cannot sway from this."

**Aimee:** Absolutely. I made the same shift in my 20s and my 30s. I worked myself to I mean, I just work myself until I'd end up in bed and then I'd lay there for about 3 months and recover from burning myself to the ground and then now I just I won't do that anymore. I had a big shift. I think COVID was a real nice time to kind of take a rest and drop back a little bit. And being able to do all the things that we do virtually now really saves us a lot of time and energy, too. 

So it's you guys we didn't have to fly to New York to interview you. What an amazing time we're living in. We can save so much time, so much money, so much carbon footprint doing these things virtually. The world has changed. And I think that we as leaders, I'm very happy to see the women that I'm interviewing also approaching leadership from a real it's okay to work hard, but it's also okay to rest and take care of ourselves and put our oxygen masks on first. I love it.

**Thyme:** Oh, that's my phrase. I love that. Yep. Because we Yes, we have to. Yep.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Too many people rely on us. Our families rely on us, and we can lead companies, but we have to leave enough of ourselves to still be there to be a leader for our families, too. So, I love what you guys are doing. I'm super impressed with you guys. What's it what's it like building a company as co-CEOs and family, and how do you divide the responsibilities and stay aligned?

**Thyme:** Yeah. Well, that's a great question. And just so you know, all your listeners know, we are cousins who had not seen each other in over 30 years. So, I had left my job and was thinking about starting this company and talking about it on social media and Denielle had walked out of her job, and reached out on to me on Facebook and said, "Hey, do you want to get together for lunch?" 

She had been driving for three years right by the exit of my house and we hadn't seen each other in over 30. And we got together for lunch that day and I told her my big dream. And I said, "Wouldn't it be absolutely amazing if we could do this together? We have such complimentary skill sets and we're family." And she said, "Absolutely not."

**Denielle:** My first response was like, "I just walked out of my job." I'm like, "What are you talking about? I'm resting. I'm taking time." And listen, I will give her credit. First of all, I'm an excellent salesperson because just fast forward within two weeks, it was a done deal. But at that time, when I first reconnected with Denielle, so first of all, it's family even though you hadn't seen each other. So, there's that built-in trust, but this woman sitting across from me, who's now the sister I've never had, hadn't been to a doctor's appointment, a dentist appointment, a mammogram in how long in your career?

**Thyme:** And it was, we just both realized that not only were there products that we needed to improve, but the workplace wasn't built for women and work schedules aren't built around women and you talk a lot about how things can be remote and we can get things done. I actually had a boss once who hired all working moms and I said, "Do you realize that we're all moms?" He goes, "Yeah, because working moms get it done." He said, "And he's right. We actually do."

And I think that's totally fair. And to talk about being we are co-founders, co-CEOs, we're cousins, we're co-cos. And people ask us about it all the time. And we both say, "I can't imagine not having a co-founder because any solopreneur out there, bless you. We give you so much love because it's hard. There are hard days and if you don't have somebody there that picks you up when you're down and you lift them up but we are pretty much on a good opposite cycle and it's we're so complimentary in our skill sets and even from day one I mean we were in a long time without any salary at all and we're packing lunches and sharing hotel rooms and I would tell you even seven years later we still share a hotel room because it's camp. It's super fun.

**Denielle:** It's the best even the smallest hotel rooms in New York City you've ever seen. But I would tell you that having a co-founder has been critical to our success. Having that family bond and trust has been absolutely critical. And what it also has done and I hope you can hear it in this interview is every time we get to a point we're like wait a minute this has got to be fun otherwise it's not worth it. We have a lot of fun together and with our team and our customers and that's really a priority because we came from pretty serious corporate backgrounds that did not have a lot of fun.

Well, and Thyme Sarah Blakely and her quote is you can do serious things without taking yourself too seriously. Thank you Thyme. But that's truly that is it we can't we took ourselves seriously for so long and now I think that's the part too is at this stage in our careers too it's you can be your authentic self. you can actually show up as you both work as well as outside as well as your social media as well as your LinkedIn what doesn't matter. It's really this is who we are and what we've recognized is actually we're actually gravitating and we're pulling people in that's who we want to work with. So it's been it's been actually it's been really good.

**Aimee:** I know I saw the video of you guys you guys ran a 5K or something dressed as tampons. Was that a thing?

**Thyme:** Thyme did Hey, we cannot afford PR. And it was awesome.

**Denielle:** Really hot and miserable, but really funny and it worked.

**Thyme:** Really funny. So funny. Actually be running the Reebok 10K for women in Boston dressed as a tampon. Ended up in Time magazine. I mean, it has been everywhere. And it got us noticed by our first buyer at Wegman's called us after she saw it, because we had been on local TV and radio and whatever. And, it changed everything. And it was really about taking risk and having courage and checking your ego at the door and having some fun. I mean, that was a that was a big turning moment for us.

**Aimee:** Yeah, that's so cool. I know. It's I love I think that there's a certain there's something that comes with being a little older where it's just a little easier to just be you. And I think that there's I think for some reason our culture has overlooked how awesome women over the age of 40 actually are.

**Thyme:** Oh yes, we are kind of awesome. We're kind of hilarious. We're funny. We're brave. We're authentic. We've been through it. We know all kinds of stuff.

**Aimee:** It's just so cool. I'm so proud of you guys. So tell me, how do you nurture company culture in a way that reflects your mission around dignity, access, and equity?

**Denielle:** I love this. And the reason I love this is and Thyme knows this about me is I live in the belief that we are in the business of humans and everything is about relationships. And so what we're doing in our work is about improving the lives of humans. What we do in the workplace is improving the lives of our employees and our team and our culture. And so it all runs parallel. It's all it is married together. 

So for this that is that's why it's super important that day-to-day we are all relationship people and I think that's one thing that has been lost in a lot of corporate America is we're forgetting that there's actually a human on the other side who you're working with and it's the reminder every day we all have things that are going on in our lives what potentially is happening when we think sometimes the stress is here it's actually not it's something that's actually happening in their life so it's really understanding that human across the table from you, but also that every day this is what we're doing. We're trying to improve the lives of this next generation that is our goal.

And if our company and our team can be exactly the same, as Thyme said too, when we thought about corporate America and what we shifted here is that creating that space of flexibility within our workforce really letting them know that we see you, we hear you, we care about you. And having that flexibility on okay, you're going to decide, one member on our team, she's got younger kids, so she's going to work until 7:00 until the kids go to bed and then she's back on. But that's just that's the we're all adults. And so it's also remembering that we've got adults that we're working with. 

So it's just really I think that the the core of this is that this is about we're working with humans. We've got to bring that back to the workplace. people are forgetting that we're not just numbers anymore. And the importance of your people are your most important asset. They really truly are. I still Aimee I just have to bring back the story of we didn't even know who you were at that point but we saw at Pepsi and Quinton was up doing his he was doing the presentation and you were filming him like the proudest mom in the entire room and it was he's doing his performance but it was the be you were beaming with pride and but that's what it is. We want to see our teams go do those things and sit there and be able to then sit back and say, "Oh my goodness, that's our people."

And that's why they they really are. They are I actually feel I know he's older than I am, but I feel like they're all my children. I really do. I've kind It's funny, but I'll come into the office and give everybody a hug. How you doing today? And that's just I'm sorry. That's the way I lead. That might not be the way that he led, but that's the way she led, right? So, that's just the way I am. I'm a big old I really am. My nickname was Mama Chicken. And I really feel like I check in with everybody just like the mama chicken. You got to cluck in. Cluck in. Cluck, cluck, cluck.

**Aimee:** So proud of Quinton that day. He absolutely crushed it. And it was such a proud moment for me and for Tomahawk to have him be on the floor at PepsiCo. It was just it was one of those moments you'll never forget.

**Denielle:** But that is that is when you can see you care about the humans and that's one of those those moments.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Yeah. No, I really really do and I'm glad you guys do too. I just think that's sort of I haven't met a woman that doesn't care about people. I mean at least the the women that I know that are mothers. It's sort of just the kind of the way we're wired. So I think that in the past it was seen as a downside as something that was not so great and now I think it's just we're building a whole new culture around a whole new corporate culture that's a more nurturing and more of a caregiving infrastructure where you see where your leader is a caregiver for all the people who work underneath them.

**Denielle:** We have to take care of our people. They are our people.

**Thyme:** Yes we do.

**Aimee:** Yep. Yeah. Yeah. So, you've installed Unicorn in places like Chase Bank and American Express and stadiums all across the country. What has scaling taught you about systems and partnership and the pace of change?

**Thyme:** That is a perfect segue from the conversation that we just had. So, as much as we talk about we're in the business of humans, what has made the difference is the relationships that we've built with our customers, with our manufacturer, with our distributors. We've been really lucky that, somebody told us early on our investors, same thing. You've got to find your people. And it was really obvious really early on when we were trying to get investors or work with any partner that wasn't aligned with our mission and our goal and our values. It's really apparent and you can feel it. 

We talk a lot about that you know it in your gut, and you know that if a door is not supposed to open to stop pushing it and to go to another door. And I think we learned that because in our old lives, we really had this this grit mentality that we were going to fight through no matter what and stay in this toxic environment or relationship or job or whatever. And now we realize some people we're not supposed to work with. They're never going to understand it. They're never going to be part of it.

And so we've curated over the years just phenomenal people all around us that we work with. And it's given us the ability to it's they they're they're working hard because they believe in what we're doing. But also, we've had to pivot a bunch of times and to have the freedom to be able to change direction because we listened because we, because we saw a problem we wanted to fix or we wanted to do something differently. You don't have that freedom in a corporate environment always to just be like, that didn't work. We're going to go do this. It's it's really been a tremendous amount of freedom and it's been wonderful to be able to curate everybody around us that we work with that has given us the ability to to scale.

**Aimee:** Yeah. It's it's really impressive. It's really impressive to watch you guys go and watch you guys grow. How do you balance innovation with operational simplicity, especially as women leaders navigating mission and margin?

**Thyme:** Yep. Well, most important is we are a for-profit company. So, that's one thing we always have to clarify with. They're like, "Oh," we're like, "No, no, no, no, no. We're not a nonprofit."

**Denielle:** It's not a hobby.

**Thyme:** No, it's not. You know what I think there's a couple places here and this is also this is just experience. You think of Thyme and I have both because we were we've been part of large corporate, we've seen how the system works in a large corporate. We understand sometimes how long a sales cycle takes. understand the the amount of people you may have to go through and the the hoops you're going to have to jump through and so and we also understand the the ups and downs of business and that goes with experience so we actually and we we used to look in the early days because we would we especially when we were out we were fundraising in the beginning and we're sitting across from 25 year-old boys I want to say boys that are at these VCs and they're looking at us like we're old ladies and they didn't actually value our experience and that's a that's actually a really important piece and an asset that we have and so I really think about that as sort of the the operational simplicity because we have that experience it's allowed us to cut out the noise.

**Thyme:** Yes.

**Denielle:** We're able to remove the fat we're not thinking about some of these other things and we've stayed so focused on the mission and look I will tell you both Thyme and I have ADHD.
